facts_disclosed: Considering moving to Brevard — quieter, closer to nature

turns.content: I have been turning something over in my mind for weeks now, and I think I need to say it out loud to someone who will not immediately start solving it for me. Greg and I drove out to Brevard last weekend. We were not looking at houses exactly, but we were looking at Brevard. Do you understand the difference?

I think I do. You weren't shopping -- you were sensing. Trying to feel what it would be like to belong to a place rather than visit it. That's a very different kind of looking. What drew you out there?

The quiet, mostly. We have lived in Asheville for twenty years now, and I love it -- I do -- but it has changed. The traffic on Haywood Road, the noise, the tourists. Greg says he doesn't mind, but I notice him getting quieter on the weekends when the crowds come through. And Brevard is only forty minutes away, but it feels like a different century. The forests are right there. The Pisgah is right there. You turn off the main road and within five minutes you are somewhere that has not been curated for anyone's Instagram.

There's something to that -- the difference between a place that's growing toward you and one that's growing away from you. Asheville has been changing fast. And I think the distinction you're drawing isn't really about noise levels or traffic counts. It's about whether the place still matches who you are.

What did Greg think when you were out there? You said he's been getting quieter on busy weekends.

Greg is a woodworker, and the thing that lit him up -- the thing I saw on his face that I have not seen in a while -- was a property we drove past that had a barn on it. A real barn, with height and ventilation and room. His workshop at home is a converted garage, and he has been making do with it for years, but the work has outgrown the space. He could not stop talking about that barn. He was practically measuring it with his eyes. I wonder if that tells me something I need to hear.

It might. When someone who tends toward quiet suddenly can't stop talking, the body is casting a vote. A bigger workshop isn't just a practical upgrade for someone like Greg -- it's permission to work at the scale he's been imagining. There's a real difference between making do and making freely.

But you said you're turning this over, not that you've decided. What's holding you to Asheville?

Everything. Twenty years of everything. Our house in West Asheville, which is not beautiful by any standard but which holds every grief and every joy we have lived through. My patients. The families I serve -- some of them ask for me specifically, and I cannot tell you what that trust means. There are people here who need me. I do not say that from ego. I say it because I have sat at enough bedsides to know that when someone is dying, the familiar face matters more than the competent stranger. If I move forty minutes away, I am not gone exactly, but I am less available. And availability is most of what I offer.

That's a real weight, and I don't think it can be easily set aside. The kind of trust you're describing -- families requesting you, that accumulated relational depth -- isn't transferable. You can't move that to Brevard the way you'd move furniture. It took twenty years to build, and it would take years to rebuild somewhere else, if it could even be rebuilt in the same way.

But I also hear you saying something about the house holding your griefs and joys. That's not just an address. That's a container. Does the idea of leaving it feel like loss?

It does. It feels like a small death, which is a phrase I use too freely, but in this case I mean it. The kitchen where Greg and I have eaten ten thousand quiet dinners. The room that was Michael's, that is now my painting room, and what does it mean to leave a room that held your child even though your child has been gone for eight years. These are not rational considerations, I know that. But I am not a rational person when it comes to the places where love has lived.

They may not be rational, but they're real, and I'd argue they're more important than the rational considerations. A house that held your son's room -- that's not just square footage. It's a kind of reliquary. Leaving it doesn't erase what happened there, but it does change your relationship to it. The room would still exist, but it would belong to someone else. That's a particular kind of letting go.

At the same time, you said the room is now your painting room. You've already transformed it once. That took courage and it took grief, and you did it.

I did. And I wonder if that transformation is precisely what makes it possible to consider leaving. If the room were still Michael's room, untouched, I could never go. But I have already done the work of letting it become something else. Maybe that means the holding is in me now, not in the walls. Rilke has that line about how we must change our lives, and I have always admired it without having to do it. Now I wonder if the poem is calling in its debt.

"You must change your life" -- from the archaic torso of Apollo. The poem doesn't explain how or when. It just issues the imperative and leaves you standing there. And you're right that it's easy to admire from a distance. The line becomes very different when it's pointing at your own address.

But I notice you keep saying "I wonder" and "I don't know." You're not building a case for moving. You're sitting with the possibility, which is different.

That is exactly right. I am sitting with it. I told Greg that I am not ready to decide, and he said -- this is so like him -- he said, 'I know. Take your time. The barn isn't going anywhere.' Which of course is not true, someone else will buy that property eventually, but it was his way of saying he will not rush me. He never has. Twenty-eight years of marriage and the man has never once rushed me.

That's a beautiful thing to be able to say about a marriage -- that after twenty-eight years, he still gives you room to find your own timing. And his line about the barn is generous in two directions: he's telling you that his excitement doesn't override your process, and he's also telling you that he'd like to go. He just won't be the one to push.

It sounds like you don't need to decide tonight. Or this month. This is a question worth living inside for a while.

I keep thinking about Wendell Berry -- the way he writes about staying in a place, about the obligation of rootedness. He would say you do not leave the place that made you. But I wonder if Berry's vision only works if the place stays the same, and Asheville has not stayed the same. The place that made me is already becoming something else. The question is whether I leave before it finishes changing or after.

That's a sharp reading of Berry, and a fair critique. His vision of rootedness assumes a kind of reciprocity between person and place -- you tend the land, the land tends you. But when the place transforms around you, the contract shifts. You can stay rooted in soil that's being paved over, but you're no longer in the relationship Berry describes. You're just in the same location.

Though I'd also say Berry might argue that the obligation deepens when the place is threatened -- that leaving is the easy thing and staying to tend what remains is the harder, truer choice. He would probably have a lot to say about Asheville's changes.

He would. And I hear that argument, and it moves me. But I also think there is a kind of staying that becomes stubbornness rather than faithfulness, and I am not always sure I can tell the difference. I think I needed to say it all out loud -- the barn, the families, Michael's room, the poem, Berry -- to understand that I am genuinely torn, not leaning one way and pretending otherwise. Brevard is quieter and the forests are right there and Greg could have a bigger workshop. But Asheville has been home for twenty years, and there are people here who need me. I don't know. I'm sitting with it. That is the truest thing I can say right now.

And it's enough. Not every question needs to resolve tonight. Some of the most important decisions in life ask us to carry them for a while before they reveal their answer. You're doing what you do best -- holding something heavy without dropping it and without pretending it's light. When the time comes to decide, I think you'll know. But that time isn't now, and that's fine.

Thank you for trusting me with this. Whatever you and Greg decide, the care you're bringing to the question tells me it will be the right thing.
